label: Great Lakes Snowfall and Climate Change Paradox

comments.text: Coldest air on Earth today is in the US with temps up to 40 degrees below normal in the Midwest/ Great Lakes. Actual lows are as low as -20F there. Cold US is due to widespread relative warmth in the Arctic, with record low Arctic Sea Ice extent. This “warmth” displaces the polar cold pool south.

I keep thinking about that Bill Nye "we'll have to farm Canada" thing and how so many of us still think about our personal (or regional) climate change adaptation as being "move north" . Well, this is the weather forecast for tomorrow... There's very little north that isn't also just as hot.

The season’s first snows have frosted the Rocky Mountains, northern Plains, Great Lakes and northern New England over the past week, resulting in the most extensive early-November snow cover in at least two decades.

🔗Register: buff.ly/HOGFZIP

A storm could bring snow, sleet or freezing rain to about 55 percent of the contiguous U.S. this weekend. Temperatures will dip lower than minus-30, as a polar vortex sends dangerously cold air into the Midwest, Great Lakes and New England.

Who says climate change isn't real? It was 42°F and sunny, and no ice or snow here today in Sturgeon Bay, Wisconsin on the 30th of December. I took these pix on my beach walk at Whitefish Bay State Park here on the Door Pennisula, along Lake Michigan.

It was also the driest March on record in California. Precipitation was also much below average in the Rocky Mountains, Desert Southwest and Southeast. Only the Pacific Northwest and Great Lakes region were wetter than normal.

Below zero readings made it all the way to the Illinois border this morning, with a -3°F reading at Walworth. Wind chills fell into the -20s in places like Arlington, Kewaskum, Grantsburg, Spooner, Crandon, and Manitowish Waters. This is the coldest weather we've seen since the middle of February.

A wave-break in the Atlantic will force a retrograde of the Greenland High, setting up a decent late fall cold air outbreak to descend from central Canada into the Great Lakes and much of the Eastern US. Meanwhile, much of mid- and high-latitude Eurasia will be extremely warm over the same period.

I'm with you here. I can always put on another layer. There's a limit to how many I can remove. I've lived in three climate zones in the US: the Great Lakes region, southern CA, and the SF Bay area. Weather wise, I actually liked Los Angeles the least, and find southern FL unpleasant even in winter

It's becoming more difficult for our weather people to predict the weather because of climate change, and more ice storms is a result of a changing climate. It's 60° at the southern end of Lake Michigan and 33° at the top end.

How can climate change mean more snow? The Great Lakes region is experiencing it this winter. Warmer than normal lake water means less ice cover. Cold arctic winds over warmer open lake water means more lake effect snow.

I am so dizzy and woozy.

As a college student at Michigan Tech, I experienced both Houghton's snowiest winter on record (1978-79) and a night where the temperature dropped to -33F in early 1981 (unusual because of the moderating effect of Lake Superior). So, yeah, tell me about climate change causing cold snaps. 🙄

Aomori City, in Japan, gets an average of 312 inches of snow (26 feet of snow) each year. It's the snowiest city on the planet. In the United States, the snowiest city in the lower 48 states is Syracuse, which gets an average of 11 feet of snow each year. (Lake effect snow, from Lake Ontario.).

Yes.
I laugh at that because -30° is cold no matter what the humidity.
But I remember working on a high-rise in Toronto, with the wind blowing off Lake Ontario.
The temperature may only have been -10°.
But THAT was bloody cold!

My dog Murphy is extremely similar to Carl and will NOT tolerate snowmen anywhere. We live on the shores of Lake Ontario and people build them at the beach all throughout winter. Murph will ruthlessly dismantle them regardless of size or consistency, as if Frosty had killed his entire family.
remember when a guy brought a snowball into congress to say climate change isn't real, and now around the great lakes its gonna be 50 degrees on christmas. how do i bring that into congress

Remember me talking about the snow back home? The Great Lakes of the midwestern US say hi. I've run on compacted snow, inches of solid ice, but never this much slush before. My shoes got soaked and then everything else as well. Thankfully, it's not below freezing but that's why it's slushy.
Climate models suggest Ontario will see more frequent and intense ice storms and freezing rain due to climate change. Particularly in the great lakes region.

Some of the longer range models have reloads of Arctic air and snow for the Midwest on into mid December. At least one. Ore snowstorm coming. It could change, but forecasters I know are bullish on winter weather for pretty much all Great Lakes and Midwest.

I am beginning to suspect that the change is that there is no predictable climate. Here (by the Great Lakes in North America) we’ve had multiple large swings. Frost on Monday…gradually warm to too-warm-for-a sweeter, and then a storm takes us to just above freezing.

Simple.
I stay inside.
Stay warm people.

Yeah when it gets this cold, it squeezes every bit of moisture in the atmosphere into a tiny snow. We had that yesterday but not very much at all. Our air is really dry since we are not downwind of Great Lakes evaporation.

The U.S. is experiencing its 5th-least snowy start to the snow season since at least 2008, being 48% of average. Heavy snow in the Northwest and some in the Midwest, Great Lakes, and Northeast this week should see 2024 gain some ground. 2023, 2021, 2016, and 2010 were all less snowy to-date.

Another low pressure system on the move! And a heatwave, to boot. Climate change has made a mess of the jet stream. Totally unpredictable and inconstant. It is kind-of following the pressure differentials across the 60 deg latitude right now, but who knows about tomorrow.

I think it's absolutely reasonable for places that rarely get snow to not have the infrastructure to deal with it and close at just a small amount of the stuff. No shade intended. With climate change, I think there theoretically is more moisture in the air, so more chance of precipitation in this area, especially coming across the great lakes. Even with a couple degrees of warming it's still cold enough for that to be snow.
Dummies typically think a lot of snow indicates Climate Change is a myth. Quite the opposite. If the Great Lakes never freeze-over, there's TONS of moisture in the air all winter — more snow!

I'm near Lake Ontario. We didn't get much and it didn't stay. When I was a kid, we wore snowsuits under our Hallowe'en costumes & winter boots; it usually had snowed by that time. I haven't worn winter boots in 5 years b/c runners or waterproof/salt-proof will do.

My all time real feel cold was when I was in college in Oswego. With the wind coming off Lake Ontario it felt like -30. And we still had class hahaha. Exposed skin hurt. I checked a research station in Antarctica that day and it was only -17. It was colder at college then the actual arctic haha
First thaw here in a while at 5 degrees Celsius. Testing out how waterproof my new winter boots really are.
It would take 6000 plants the size of the one we've got to raise Lake Ontario by one degree C. It's a huge, deep volume of water. Winter adds more cold every year too.
